content:
# Genesis Chapter 26 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 14v–15r | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_026_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_026_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 22–23 | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209

---

**1** Now there was a famine in the land, besides the first famine that was in the days of Abraham. And Isaac went to Gerar, to Abimelech king of the Philistines.

**2** And the LORD appeared to him and said, "Do not go down to Egypt; dwell in the land of which I shall tell you.

**3** Sojourn in this land, and I will be with you and will bless you, for to you and to your offspring I will give all these lands, and I will establish the oath that I swore to Abraham your father.

**4** I will multiply your offspring as the stars of heaven and will give to your offspring all these lands. And in your offspring all the nations of the earth shall bless themselves,

**5** because Abraham obeyed my voice and kept my charge, my commandments, my statutes, and my laws."

**6** So Isaac settled in Gerar.

**7** When the men of the place asked him about his wife, he said, "She is my sister," for he was afraid to say, "My wife," thinking, "lest the men of the place should kill me because of Rebekah," for she was attractive in appearance.

**8** When he had been there a long time, Abimelech king of the Philistines looked out through a window and saw — and behold, Isaac was laughing with Rebekah his wife.

**9** So Abimelech called Isaac and said, "Behold, she is your wife. How then have you said, 'She is my sister'?" Isaac said to him, "Because I thought, 'Lest I die because of her.'"

**10** Abimelech said, "What is this you have done to us? One of the people might easily have lain with your wife, and you would have brought guilt upon us."

**11** So Abimelech warned all the people, saying, "Whoever touches this man or his wife shall surely be put to death."

**12** And Isaac sowed in that land and reaped in the same year a hundredfold. The LORD blessed him,

**13** and the man became great and kept on growing until he became very great.

**14** He had possessions of flocks and herds and a great household, so that the Philistines envied him.

**15** Now all the wells that his father's servants had dug in the days of Abraham his father, the Philistines had stopped them up and filled them with earth.

**16** And Abimelech said to Isaac, "Go away from us, for you are much mightier than we."

**17** So Isaac departed from there and encamped in the Valley of Gerar and settled there.

**18** And Isaac dug again the wells of water that had been dug in the days of Abraham his father, which the Philistines had stopped up after the death of Abraham. And he gave them the names that his father had given them.

**19** But when Isaac's servants dug in the valley and found there a well of spring water,

**20** the herdsmen of Gerar quarreled with Isaac's herdsmen, saying, "The water is ours." So he called the name of the well Esek, because they contended with him.

**21** Then they dug another well, and they quarreled over that also, so he called its name Sitnah.

**22** And he moved from there and dug another well, and they did not quarrel over it. So he called its name Rehoboth, saying, "For now the LORD has made room for us, and we shall be fruitful in the land."

**23** From there he went up to Beer-sheba.

**24** And the LORD appeared to him the same night and said, "I am the God of Abraham your father. Fear not, for I am with you and will bless you and multiply your offspring for my servant Abraham's sake."

**25** So he built an altar there and called upon the name of the LORD and pitched his tent there. And there Isaac's servants dug a well.

**26** When Abimelech went to him from Gerar with Ahuzzath his advisor and Phicol the commander of his army,

**27** Isaac said to them, "Why have you come to me, seeing that you hate me and have sent me away from you?"

**28** They said, "We have plainly seen that the LORD has been with you. So we said, 'Let there now be an oath between us, between you and us, and let us make a covenant with you,

**29** that you will do us no harm, just as we have not touched you and have done to you nothing but good and have sent you away in peace. You are now the blessed of the LORD.'"

**30** So he made them a feast, and they ate and drank.

**31** In the morning they rose early and exchanged oaths. And Isaac sent them on their way, and they departed from him in peace.

**32** That same day Isaac's servants came and told him about the well that they had dug and said to him, "We have found water."

**33** He called it Shibah; therefore the name of the city is Beer-sheba to this day.

**34** When Esau was forty years old, he took Judith the daughter of Beeri the Hittite and Basemath the daughter of Elon the Hittite to be his wives.

**35** They were a bitterness of spirit to Isaac and to Rebekah.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Source Manuscripts

This translation was produced from direct reading of four manuscript image sources:

1. **Leningrad Codex (Codex Leningradensis, B19A)**, dated c. 1008 CE. West Semitic Research Project photographs by Bruce E. Zuckerman. Genesis 26 spans Folios 14v–15r across two pages.

   **Folio 14v** (GEN_026_page_001.jpg): The verso of Folio 14, presented in inverted orientation per WSRP imaging convention. The page carries the close of Genesis 25 at its top (the birthright scene ending at v. 34) and then transitions into Genesis 26 after a parashah break. The three-column Tiberian format continues with full Masoretic pointing and cantillation. This folio carries Genesis 26:1 through approximately 26:22, encompassing the famine and divine charge to Isaac, the settlement in Gerar, the wife-sister deception with Rebekah and Abimelech, Isaac's agricultural and pastoral prosperity, the Philistines' envy, the stopped wells, Abimelech's expulsion of Isaac, and the first two well-name episodes (Esek and Sitnah).

   **Folio 15r** (GEN_026_page_002.jpg): The recto of Folio 15, presented in normal orientation with the folio number visible in the upper margin. This page carries Genesis 26:23 through 26:35, and the beginning of Genesis 27 is visible at the bottom of the page. The text covers: the third well-name Rehoboth, Isaac's departure to Beer-sheba, the divine appearance and night oracle, Isaac's altar and well, the arrival of Abimelech's delegation, the covenant feast and oath exchange, the well-naming Shibah and the Beer-sheba aetiology, and the closing notice of Esau's Hittite marriages.

2. **Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209)** — Septuagint/LXX, 4th century CE. Two pages were read directly.

   **Page 22** (GEN_026_vaticanus_page_001.jpg): Vaticanus page 22 (digi.vatlib.it page pa_0022). The chapter marker "CAP. XXIX" is visible in the left margin, reflecting the LXX's chapter division. The page carries the end of Genesis 25 (Rebekah's oracle, the twin birth, the birthright scene) and transitions into Genesis 26. The Genesis 26 material on this page covers the famine notice, the LORD's appearance and charge to Isaac, the Gerar settlement, the wife-sister episode, and Isaac's agricultural prosperity. The continuous three-column uncial text of Vaticanus is legible throughout.

   **Page 23** (GEN_026_vaticanus_page_002.jpg): Vaticanus page 23 (digi.vatlib.it page pa_0023). The chapter marker "CAP. XXX" is visible at the lower left, marking the LXX's division into what becomes Genesis 27. This page carries the remainder of Genesis 26: the well disputes, the Rehoboth naming, Beer-sheba, the divine appearance, the covenant with Abimelech, the Shibah aetiology, and Esau's marriages. The beginning of Genesis 27 (the Jacob-Esau-blessing narrative) is visible at the very bottom of the page.

---

### Key Translation Decisions

**1. "Besides the First Famine" (v. 1)**

The narrator opens with a clarifying aside: זוּלָת הָרָעָב הָרִאשׁוֹן אֲשֶׁר הָיָה בִּימֵי אַבְרָהָם (zulat hara'av harishon asher hayah bimei Avraham — "apart from / besides the first famine that was in the days of Abraham"). This is a rare piece of narrative bracketing — the narrator steps outside the story to orient the reader historically, linking this famine to Genesis 12:10, when Abraham descended to Egypt for the same reason. The formal translation renders "besides the first famine that was in the days of Abraham," preserving the aside and its cross-reference. The LXX (Vaticanus) renders χωρὶς τοῦ λιμοῦ τοῦ προτέρου ὅς ἐγένετο ἐν τῷ καιρῷ τῷ Αβρααμ ("apart from the earlier famine that came to pass in the time of Abraham"), following the MT exactly in structure and sense.

**2. אֶהְיֶה עִמְּךָ — "I Will Be with You" (v. 3)**

The divine promise אֶהְיֶה עִמְּךָ (ehyeh immekha — "I will be with you") uses the first-person imperfect of the verb הָיָה (hayah, "to be"). This is the same verbal root from which the divine name YHWH (יְהוָה) is derived and the same form YHWH uses at the burning bush — אֶהְיֶה אֲשֶׁר אֶהְיֶה (ehyeh asher ehyeh — "I AM WHO I AM / I WILL BE WHAT I WILL BE," Exodus 3:14). The Isaacite covenant promise thus carries an echo — perhaps deliberate — of the most charged divine self-disclosure in the OT. The formal translation renders simply "I will be with you," allowing the English reader who knows Exodus to hear the resonance without forcing it. The LXX renders ἔσομαι μετά σου (esomai meta sou — "I will be with you"), using the future indicative of εἰμί — the standard LXX rendering of the 'ehyeh formula, without the Septuagintal ego eimi ("I AM") that appears at Exodus 3:14.

**3. וְהִתְבָּרֲכוּ — "Shall Bless Themselves" (v. 4)**

The verb in v. 4 is וְהִתְבָּרֲכוּ בְזַרְעֲךָ כֹּל גּוֹיֵי הָאָרֶץ (vehitbarakhú vezar'akha kol goyei ha'arets). The Hithpael (reflexive-causative) of בָּרַך (barakh, "to bless") means literally "they shall bless themselves." This reflexive form recurs in the Abrahamic covenant texts (Genesis 12:3; 18:18; 22:18; 26:4; 28:14) and sits at the center of a longstanding debate: does the Hithpael mean "shall bless themselves [by invoking your name]" or "shall be blessed [through you / as a result of you]"? The parallel verse Genesis 22:18 uses the Niphal (נִבְרְכוּ, nivrekhú — passive: "shall be blessed"), which Paul cites in Galatians 3:8 in support of the passive-universal-blessing reading. Here the Hithpael is used, which in its grammatical form is reflexive. The formal translation renders "shall bless themselves" — preserving the Hithpael's reflexive force and the slight but real distinction from the Niphal forms in Genesis 12 and 22. The LXX (Vaticanus) renders ἐνευλογηθήσονται ἐν τῷ σπέρματί σου πάντα τὰ ἔθνη τῆς γῆς ("all the nations of the earth shall be blessed in your seed"), using the future passive — essentially harmonizing the Hithpael to the Niphal and resolving the ambiguity in the universalist direction.

**4. מְצַחֵק — "Laughing With" (v. 8)**

Verse 8 contains the narrative's most significant wordplay. The verb is מְצַחֵק (metsacheq — Piel participle of צָחַק, tsachaq), meaning "laughing, playing, fondling, sporting with." Abimelech looks through a window and sees Isaac מְצַחֵק אֵת רִבְקָה אִשְׁתּוֹ (metsacheq et Rivqah ishto — "laughing with / caressing Rebekah his wife"). The root צָחַק is the root of the name יִצְחָק (Yitzhak — "Isaac"), whose name means "he laughs / he will laugh." The narrator thus signals the deception's unraveling through the protagonist's own name: the man named Laughter was caught laughing — and that laughter revealed what his sister-claim concealed. The Piel of tsachaq can shade from innocent laughter and play to more intimate physical expression; in Genesis 39:14 and 39:17 it is used by Potiphar's wife to mean sexual dalliance. The formal translation renders "Isaac was laughing with Rebekah his wife" — preserving the verb (not paraphrasing to "caressing" or "fondling") while including "his wife" as the narrator's revelation that forces the meaning. The wordplay cannot be fully reproduced in English, but the translation preserves its verbal form. The LXX renders παίζοντα μετά Ρεββεκκας τῆς γυναικὸς αὐτοῦ (paidzonta meta Rhebbeka tēs gynaikos autou — "playing with Rebekah his wife"), using παίζω (paizō, "to play, to sport") — a word that can also carry erotic overtones in Greek. The formal translation follows the MT's tsachaq rather than the LXX's paizō in order to preserve the Isaacic name-echo.

**5. מֵאָה שְׁעָרִים — "A Hundredfold" (v. 12)**

The phrase מֵאָה שְׁעָרִים (me'ah she'arim) is literally "a hundred gates / measures / units." The word שַׁעַר (sha'ar) in its plural construct form here functions as a measure of yield — the phrase means "a hundred (sha'arim) [of yield for each unit sown]," i.e., a hundredfold return. This is an extraordinary agricultural yield — ancient Near Eastern farming typically returned fivefold to tenfold — and marks Isaac's prosperity as divinely superabundant. The phrase is striking because שַׁעַר normally means "gate" (as in city gate), and its secondary sense as "measure" is relatively rare. The formal translation renders "a hundredfold," following the traditional English idiom (KJV, ESV, NASB), which captures the agricultural sense without attempting to render the Hebrew's unusual term literally. The LXX renders ἑκατοστεύουσαν κριθήν ("a hundredfold of barley"), adding "barley" as an explanatory gloss not in the MT. The formal translation follows the MT.

**6. עֵשֶׂק / שִׂטְנָה / רְחֹבוֹת — "Esek / Sitnah / Rehoboth" (vv. 20–22)**

The three well-names form a theological sequence: Esek (עֵשֶׂק, "contention, strife"), Sitnah (שִׂטְנָה, "accusation, hostility, enmity"), Rehoboth (רְחֹבוֹת, "wide places, room, expanses"). The progression moves from conflict through intensified opposition to final resolution: the LORD "has made room (הִרְחִיב, hirchiv — root רָחַב, rachav) for us." Notably, Sitnah (שִׂטְנָה) shares its root with שָׂטָן (satan — "adversary, accuser"), giving the second well-name a darker resonance than mere human quarreling. The formal translation preserves all three well-names transliterated, following the convention established in Genesis 25 for proper names, and renders the etymological explanations within the verse text as provided by the narrative. The LXX (Vaticanus) transliterates similarly: Αδικία ("Injustice/Wrong" — interpreting rather than transliterating Esek), Εχθρία ("Enmity/Hostility" — interpreting Sitnah), Ευρυχωρία ("Wide-Space/Spaciousness" — interpreting Rehoboth). The LXX's choice to interpret rather than transliterate these names makes explicit what the Hebrew leaves as wordplay. The formal translation retains the transliterated Hebrew names.

**7. אֶהְיֶה עִמְּךָ — "I Am with You" (v. 24)**

The divine appearance at Beer-sheba in v. 24 repeats the 'ehyeh promise of v. 3, now in the perfect-present tense: אָנֹכִי אִתְּךָ (anokhi ittekha — "I [am] with you"). The formal formula here is אַל תִּירָא כִּי אִתְּךָ אָנֹכִי (al tira ki ittekha anokhi — "Fear not, for I am with you"). This phrase — "Fear not, for I am with you" — becomes one of the signature divine assurances in the OT prophetic and narrative tradition (cf. Isaiah 41:10; 43:5; Jeremiah 1:8, 1:19; 30:11). The formal translation renders "Fear not, for I am with you," preserving the word order and the direct address.

**8. מְרֵעֵהוּ — "His Advisor / Friend" (v. 26)**

Abimelech arrives accompanied by מֵרֵעֵהוּ אַחֻזַּת (me're'ehu Achuzzat). The word מֵרֵעֵהוּ (me're'ehu) is from רֵעַ (re'a, "friend, companion, neighbor") with a prefixed mem indicating "from among his friends/companions." In the context of a royal delegation, this is more likely a titled position — "his advisor, his companion, his confidant" — rather than simply a personal friend. Some translations render "his best man" (NAB) or "his personal adviser" (NIV). The formal translation renders "his advisor," which captures the official character of the delegation while remaining faithful to the root. The LXX renders Αχοζαθ ὁ νυμφαγωγός αὐτοῦ (Achozath ho nymphagōgos autou — "Achuzzath his groomsman/friend of the bridegroom"), using νυμφαγωγός (nymphagōgos, literally "one who leads the bride"), which in classical Greek can mean a close male companion or groomsman. The formal translation's "advisor" is closer to the diplomatic context.

**9. שִׁבְעָה / שְׁבוּעָה — "Shibah" and the Beer-sheba Aetiology (v. 33)**

The well-name in v. 33 is שִׁבְעָה (Shivah), which the text presents as the basis for the place-name בְּאֵר שָׁבַע (Be'er Sheva — "Beer-sheba"). The word שִׁבְעָה (shivah) can derive from either: (a) שֶׁבַע (sheva, "seven") — making Beer-sheba "Well of Seven," or (b) שְׁבוּעָה (shevu'ah, "oath, sworn agreement") — making Beer-sheba "Well of the Oath." Both etymologies have been current since antiquity. Genesis 21:28–31, the first Beer-sheba aetiology involving Abraham and Abimelech, plays on both: seven ewe lambs are given as witness (establishing the "seven" connection), and the place is called "Oath/Seven Well" because there they swore an oath. Now in Genesis 26, a second Beer-sheba aetiology is offered, this time centered on Isaac's covenant with a later Abimelech: the well they dug is called Shibah ("Oath/Seven"), and therefore the city is Beer-sheba. The formal translation renders the well-name "Shibah" (transliterated) and allows the English rendering to let the reader perceive the connection. The LXX renders the name as Ορκος (Horkos — "Oath"), choosing the oath-etymology over the number-etymology. The formal translation follows the MT's transliterated form.

**10. מֹרַת רוּחַ — "A Bitterness of Spirit" (v. 35)**

The closing verse uses the phrase מֹרַת רוּחַ (morat ruach — literally "bitterness of spirit"). The root מָרַר (marar, "to be bitter") generates the abstract noun מֹרַת (morat, "bitterness"). The phrase qualifies the emotional impact of Esau's Hittite marriages on Isaac and Rebekah: they were a bitterness of spirit to them — not merely inconvenient or disappointing, but a deep, cutting grief. The word מָרַר is the same root used for the bitterness of Israel's slavery in Egypt (Exodus 1:14 — וַיְמָרֲרוּ אֶת חַיֵּיהֶם, "they made their lives bitter"). The formal translation renders "a bitterness of spirit," preserving the genitive construct and the full weight of the idiom. The LXX renders ἐρίζουσαι (erizousai — "quarreling, contentious women"), which is a paraphrase interpreting the source of the bitterness rather than rendering the emotional state of Isaac and Rebekah. The formal translation follows the MT.

**11. "The LORD" Throughout**

The Tetragrammaton יְהוָה (YHWH) appears seven times in Genesis 26 (vv. 2, 12, 22, 24 [×2], 25, 29). It is rendered "the LORD" in small capitals throughout, consistent with the formal convention established across this translation. This follows the ancient substitution tradition (Adonai in reading, κύριος in the LXX) and the standard formal English Bible tradition.

---

### LXX (Vaticanus) Variants — Genesis 26

Pages 22–23 of Codex Vaticanus were read directly. The LXX's continuous uncial text was legible across both pages. The chapter division markers "CAP. XXIX" (page 22) and "CAP. XXX" (page 23) reflect the LXX's chapter system. Key variants observed:

| Verse | MT (Leningrad Codex) | LXX (Vaticanus) | Translation Decision | Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 26:1 | זוּלָת הָרָעָב הָרִאשׁוֹן ("besides the first famine") | χωρὶς τοῦ λιμοῦ τοῦ προτέρου ("apart from the earlier famine") | "besides the first famine" | MT and LXX agree in sense; formal translation follows MT's "first" (rishon) rather than LXX's "earlier" (proteros) |
| 26:2 | פְּלִשְׁתִּים ("Philistines") | Φυλιστιιμ | "Philistines" | Standard rendering |
| 26:4 | וְהִתְבָּרֲכוּ (Hithpael — "shall bless themselves") | ἐνευλογηθήσονται (future passive — "shall be blessed") | "shall bless themselves" | LXX harmonizes to Niphal forms elsewhere (Gen 12, 22); MT Hithpael preserved |
| 26:5 | מִשְׁמַרְתִּי מִצְוֹתַי חֻקּוֹתַי וְתוֹרֹתָי ("my charge, my commandments, my statutes, and my laws") | τὰ προστάγματά μου καὶ τὰς ἐντολάς μου καὶ τὰ δικαιώματά μου καὶ τὰ νόμιμά μου ("my ordinances, my commandments, my righteous requirements, my laws") | "my charge, my commandments, my statutes, and my laws" | MT's four-fold enumeration preserved in full; LXX matches in four terms |
| 26:7 | טוֹבַת מַרְאֶה הִיא ("she was attractive in appearance") | ὡραία τῇ ὄψει ("beautiful in appearance") | "attractive in appearance" | MT's טוֹב מַרְאֶה ("good of appearance") rendered "attractive"; LXX uses ὡραία ("lovely, beautiful, in her prime") |
| 26:8 | מְצַחֵק (Piel — "laughing/caressing") | παίζοντα ("playing, sporting") | "laughing with" | MT's tsachaq (name-echo of Isaac) preserved; LXX's paizō loses the wordplay |
| 26:10 | כִּמְעַט ("easily, almost") — "one of the people might almost have lain" | μικρόν ("a little") | "might easily have lain" | Both MT and LXX convey near-catastrophe; formal translation renders the force of the statement |
| 26:12 | מֵאָה שְׁעָרִים ("a hundredfold") | ἑκατοστεύουσαν κριθήν ("a hundredfold of barley") | "a hundredfold" | LXX adds "barley" as an explanatory gloss not in MT; formal translation follows MT |
| 26:20 | עֵשֶׂק ("Esek/Contention") | Αδικία ("Injustice") | "Esek" | LXX interprets; formal translation retains Hebrew name |
| 26:21 | שִׂטְנָה ("Sitnah/Hostility") | Εχθρία ("Enmity") | "Sitnah" | LXX interprets; formal translation retains Hebrew name |
| 26:22 | רְחֹבוֹת ("Rehoboth/Wide Places") | Ευρυχωρία ("Spaciousness") | "Rehoboth" | LXX interprets; formal translation retains Hebrew name |
| 26:24 | אַל תִּירָא ("Fear not") | μὴ φοβοῦ ("do not be afraid") | "Fear not" | Exact equivalents |
| 26:26 | מֵרֵעֵהוּ ("his advisor/friend") | ὁ νυμφαγωγός αὐτοῦ ("his groomsman/companion") | "his advisor" | LXX's nymphagōgos is interpretive; formal translation follows MT's re'a sense in royal context |
| 26:33 | שִׁבְעָה ("Shibah" — oath/seven) | Ορκος ("Oath") | "Shibah" | LXX chooses the oath-etymology; formal translation retains transliterated name |
| 26:35 | מֹרַת רוּחַ ("bitterness of spirit") | ἐρίζουσαι ("quarreling women") | "a bitterness of spirit" | LXX interprets the source of bitterness rather than rendering the MT's emotional state; formal translation follows MT |

---

### Structural and Theological Notes

**Genesis 26 as the Isaac Chapter**

As the manifest notes, Genesis 26 is the only chapter in all of Genesis focused entirely on Isaac as the narrative protagonist. The patriarchal cycle gives Abraham twenty-five chapters and Jacob (including the Joseph story) twenty-five more; Isaac receives essentially this single chapter as his own story. The effect is to present Isaac as a figure who re-enacts his father's experiences in miniature: the famine, the wife-sister deception, the divine covenant renewal, the well disputes, the covenant with Abimelech, the Beer-sheba altar. Isaac is, in some sense, a typological repetition of Abraham — not a diminished figure, but a deliberately drawn echo. The formal translation preserves this pattern without flattening it into Abraham's narrative.

**The Divine Covenant in Three Stages (vv. 2–5, 24)**

The LORD's appearance to Isaac in vv. 2–5 is the most expansive divine speech in the chapter. It is notable for its Abrahamic grounding: every element of the promise made to Isaac — land, offspring, nations blessed — is explicitly rooted in Abraham. "I will establish the oath that I swore to Abraham your father" (v. 3); "for my servant Abraham's sake" (v. 24). Isaac does not receive the covenant as an independent figure; he receives it as Abraham's son. The formal translation preserves this father-grounding throughout, rendering each phrase of the divine speeches without smoothing their accumulated covenantal weight.

**The Wife-Sister Narrative — Third Instance**

Genesis 26:7–11 is the third wife-sister episode in Genesis (after Genesis 12:10–20 with Abraham and Pharaoh, and Genesis 20:1–18 with Abraham and Abimelech). All three share a structural pattern: patriarch claims wife as sister out of fear; the deception is discovered; the patriarch is rebuked; the patriarch departs with his household intact. Genesis 26 is distinctive in that: (a) there is no divine intervention to warn or afflict Abimelech — the deception is discovered through natural observation; (b) Abimelech's rebuke is moral and clear: "you would have brought guilt upon us" (v. 10); (c) Abimelech acts protectively once the truth is known, issuing a death-penalty proclamation to protect Isaac and Rebekah. The formal translation preserves the dry, unhurried narrative pace of this episode without either excusing Isaac's deception or melodramatizing Abimelech's response.

**The Three Wells as Narrative Arc**

The well-dispute sequence (vv. 19–22) follows a dramatic arc that mirrors the covenant structure of the chapter. Esek (contention) and Sitnah (hostility) represent human opposition to Isaac's divinely-given prosperity. Rehoboth ("the LORD has made room for us") resolves the conflict not through human victory but through divine provision of space. This is the narrative's theological statement: the covenant people's inheritance comes not by conquest but by the LORD enlarging the land for them. The name Rehoboth recalls the root רָחַב (rachav, "broad, wide") that appears in the Abrahamic land promise (Genesis 13:17 — "the length and breadth of the land"). The formal translation renders the well-naming speeches in full, preserving the theological-geographical wordplay.

**Esau's Marriages as Structural Frame (vv. 34–35)**

The chapter closes with a two-verse notice of Esau's Hittite marriages that functions as a structural frame-connector: it looks back to the wife-sister concern that runs through the chapter (exogamous marriages to Canaanite women are the problem Genesis 24 was designed to avoid for Isaac; now Esau repeats the danger at a higher level) and forward to Genesis 27–28 (Rebekah's stated reason for sending Jacob to Paddan-aram is precisely to avoid Esau's error: "What good will my life be to me if Jacob marries one of the Hittite women?" — Genesis 27:46). The phrase "bitterness of spirit" (מֹרַת רוּחַ, morat ruach) is the narrator's emotional verdict — the same unsparing finality as "Esau despised his birthright" in 25:34. The formal translation renders it without softening.
